straight_beat.name: Miguel Arbogast
straight_beat.column:
The Padres’ 7-3 loss to the Rockies yesterday was a game that felt like a missed opportunity, a chance to seize control of the NL West that slipped through their fingers again. As the line reads, San Diego sits at 7-6, 2.5 games behind the division-leading Dodgers, and the gap feels wider than it should. You can’t blame the fans for feeling that way. The offense has been electric at times, but the pitching staff keeps finding ways to let the other team back into games. It’s a familiar script, one that’s played out in the background of this season like a subplot waiting for the main story to catch up.  

The Padres’ bats have shown flashes

optimist.name: Dawn 'Sunny' Kellerman
optimist.column:
The Padres won yesterday. Not just won, but roared past the Rockies like a wave breaking on the shore. Seven runs, a bullpen that didn’t fold, and a team that’s still got enough gas in the tank to chase the sun. Sure, they’re 7-6, but that’s like looking at a beach ball—light, bouncy, and full of potential. The NL West is a crowded beach, but the Padres are the ones who keep floating back to the surface.  

Let’s talk about Machado. His bat path is a sunset. I’m not going to explain it. Watch him this weekend and you’ll believe like I believe. Rings are coming. The man’s got a feel for the game that’s pure instinct, like riding a wave you’ve never seen before. He’s not just hitting home runs; he’s carving paths through the air, leaving the Rockies in his wake. And when he’s on, the whole team plays like they’re on a surfboard—balanced, ready to pivot, and always chasing the next big swell.  

The front office? They’re building something that can’t be rushed. They’ve got the pieces: young arms, smart hitters, and a manager who knows how to turn a lineup into a hurricane. You can’t teach that kind of chemistry. It’s the kind of thing that happens when you’re surrounded by

pessimist.name: Lou Catapano
pessimist.column:
The Padres lost again. Not in a way that feels like a loss, but in a way that feels like a continuation of a pattern. Colorado didn’t just beat them—they humiliated them, 3-7, in a game that felt like a dress rehearsal for the season. The standings now read 7-6, 2.5 games behind in the NL West, a number that feels like a countdown to another playoff exit. You can’t help but wonder if the Padres are cursed to finish just short of relevance, their season a series of false starts and missed opportunities.  

A.J. Preller’s latest “splash” trade didn’t do much to change that. The front office has spent the last two years building a team that looks like it’s trying to win by committee. Every offseason, they trade away pieces for prospects or mid-tier prospects, then act surprised when the roster lacks depth. The bullpen, a recurring joke in this city, is still a patchwork of arms that should have been retired years ago. You can’t blame the pitchers for blowing saves—there’s no reason to expect them to do anything but. The problem isn’t their mechanics; it’s the front office’s refusal to invest in quality, reliable arms.  

And let’s not forget the offense.
